name: Aloe Vera
nativeRange: Arabian Peninsula, likely Oman and Yemen
habitat: Arid and semi-arid regions; requires well-drained soils and warm, dry climates

description.overall appearance: Succulent perennial forming rosettes of thick, fleshy leaves 12-24 inches long, with mature plants reaching 24-36 inches in height and width.
description.roots: Shallow, spreading root system with limited depth but extensive lateral spread to capture surface water.
description.stem: Short, thick stem at base of rosette, often not visible above ground level.
description.leaves: Thick, fleshy, lance-shaped leaves 12-24 inches long and 2-4 inches wide, blue-green to gray-green with serrated margins and white spots in young plants.
description.flowers: Tubular yellow to orange flowers arranged in dense racemes on tall stalks 24-36 inches high; blooms in summer in mature plants (3+ years).
description.fruits/seeds: Triangular capsules containing numerous flat, winged seeds; rarely produces viable seeds in cultivation.

partsUsed: Leaf latex (yellow bitter liquid from outer layer)
traditionalUses: Ancient Egyptian medicine for wound healing, skin conditions
Traditional Chinese Medicine for heat clearing, liver support
Folk medicine worldwide for burns, cuts, skin inflammation
modernUses: Burn treatment and wound healing acceleration
preparations: Direct application from freshly cut leaf
Fresh gel blended with water for internal use
Concentrated gel powder for internal use
Gel infused in carrier oils for topical use
Gel incorporated into topical preparations
contraindications: Known allergy to plants in Liliaceae family
sideEffects: Digestive upset with internal latex use
Electrolyte imbalances with chronic latex use
Potential kidney damage with long-term latex use
drugInteractions: Diabetes medications (may enhance blood sugar lowering)

name: Angelica
nativeRange: Northern Europe, Iceland, Greenland, and Siberia
habitat: Moist soils near streams, in meadows, and mountainous regions in cool, temperate climates

description.overall appearance: Angelica is a tall biennial or short-lived perennial herb growing 4-8 feet tall with large compound leaves and distinctive umbrella-shaped flower clusters.
description.roots: Large, thick taproot is aromatic and can reach 12 inches long. The root is the most medicinally valued part of the plant.
description.stem: Hollow, thick stems are deeply grooved and can reach 2 inches in diameter. Stems are often purplish and covered with a whitish bloom.
description.leaves: Large, compound leaves are divided into toothed leaflets. Leaves can reach 3 feet long with inflated sheaths at the base.
description.flowers: Tiny white to greenish flowers are arranged in large, compound umbels (umbrella-shaped clusters) up to 10 inches across.
description.fruits/seeds: Flattened, oval fruits split into two parts when ripe. Seeds are aromatic and have been used as spice.

modernUses: Flavoring agent in liqueurs and perfumes
preparations: 1 tsp dried root per cup water, simmer 10-15 minutes
1:5 ratio in 60% alcohol, 2-4 ml three times daily
1 tsp dried leaves per cup water, steep 10 minutes
Root infused in oil for topical applications
Traditional European confection
sideEffects: Possible skin irritation in sensitive individuals
drugInteractions: May interact with blood-thinning medications (coumarin content)
May enhance effects of photosensitizing drugs

name: Arnica
nativeRange: Mountains of Europe and western North America
habitat: Mountain meadows, moorlands, and acidic grasslands at high elevations

description.overall appearance: Arnica is a perennial herb growing 1-2 feet tall with a basal rosette of leaves and distinctive bright yellow daisy-like flowers.
description.roots: Creeping rhizome system spreads horizontally underground, producing new shoots and forming colonies in suitable habitat.
description.stem: Erect, hairy flower stems arise from basal rosette, typically bearing 1-3 flower heads at the top.
description.leaves: Basal rosette of oval leaves 2-3 inches long with parallel veins. Leaves are light green, hairy, and arranged in opposite pairs.
description.flowers: Bright golden-yellow flower heads are 2-3 inches across with 10-15 ray petals around a central disc of tubular flowers.
description.fruits/seeds: Small achenes (seeds) are topped with white bristly pappus that aids in wind dispersal.

preparations: 1:10 ratio in 50% alcohol for external use only
Flowers infused in carrier oil for massage
Traditional preparation with beeswax and oils
Diluted tincture applied on cloth to affected area
Highly diluted preparations for internal use
drugInteractions: No significant interactions with topical use
Avoid internal use which could interact with medications
May enhance effects of other topical anti-inflammatory preparations

name: Ashwagandha
nativeRange: India, Pakistan, Afghanistan, and Sri Lanka
habitat: Dry regions, waste lands, and cultivated fields; adapts to poor soils and arid conditions at elevations up to 5,000 feet

description.overall appearance: Small, woody shrub growing 1-3 feet tall with oval leaves and small, greenish-yellow flowers followed by orange-red berries enclosed in papery calyces.
description.roots: Thick, fleshy, unbranched taproot that can grow 12-18 inches long; roots are cream to light brown externally, white internally, with a horse-like odor when fresh.
description.stem: Branching, woody stems covered with fine, star-shaped hairs (stellate pubescence); stems are green when young, becoming brown and woody with age.
description.leaves: Simple, oval leaves 2-4 inches long, arranged alternately; leaves are covered with fine hairs and have a dull green color with lighter undersides.
description.flowers: Small, inconspicuous, bell-shaped flowers that are greenish-yellow; flowers grow in clusters in leaf axils and bloom throughout the year in suitable climates.
description.fruits/seeds: Small, round, orange-red berries enclosed in an inflated, papery calyx; berries contain numerous small, yellow seeds and ripen 6-8 months after flowering.

traditionalUses: General tonic and rejuvenative (Rasayana in Ayurveda)
preparations: 1-6 grams root powder daily, traditionally mixed with milk and honey
3-6 grams dried root per cup water, simmer 15-20 minutes
1:5 ratio in 45% alcohol, 2-4 ml twice daily
300-500mg daily containing 1.5-12% withanolides
Traditional powder preparation with other herbs
sideEffects: Drowsiness or sedation (especially with high doses)
Potential liver toxicity with very high doses
drugInteractions: Sedatives and sleep medications (may enhance effects)
Blood pressure medications (may lower blood pressure)

name: Astragalus
nativeRange: Northern China, Mongolia, Korea
habitat: Grasslands, scrublands, and open woodlands; prefers well-drained soils and temperate climates at elevations up to 8,000 feet

description.overall appearance: Perennial herbaceous plant growing 1-3 feet tall with pinnately compound leaves and yellow pea-like flowers; develops a deep, thick taproot that is the primary medicinal part.
description.roots: Large, thick taproot that can grow 2-3 feet long and 1-2 inches in diameter; roots are yellowish-white with a sweet taste and fibrous texture when dried.
description.stem: Erect, branching stems that are slightly hairy and hollow; stems are green to reddish-brown and can reach 2-4 feet in height with multiple branches.
description.leaves: Pinnately compound leaves with 12-18 pairs of small, oval leaflets; leaves are alternate and covered with fine hairs; leaflets are 1/2 to 1 inch long.
description.flowers: Small, yellow pea-like flowers arranged in dense, spike-like clusters (racemes); flowers bloom from June to August and are followed by inflated seed pods.
description.fruits/seeds: Inflated, membranous seed pods (legumes) about 1 inch long containing several kidney-shaped seeds; pods are hairy and bladder-like when mature.

partsUsed: Roots (primary medicinal part, harvested after 4-7 years)
Occasionally young aerial parts in traditional preparations
modernUses: Diabetes management and blood sugar control
preparations: 9-30 grams dried root per day, simmered 30-60 minutes
1-2 teaspoons powdered root per cup hot water, steep 15 minutes
1:5 ratio in 40% alcohol, 3-5 ml three times daily
1-3 grams powdered root daily in water or food
Traditional Chinese preparation with other herbs and foods

sideEffects: Generally well-tolerated with minimal side effects
Rare allergic reactions in sensitive individuals
Mild gastrointestinal upset in some users
drugInteractions: Diabetes medications (may enhance blood sugar lowering)

name: Basil
nativeRange: Central Africa to Southeast Asia, now cultivated worldwide
habitat: Warm, temperate climates; well-drained soils; full sun to partial shade

description.overall appearance: Annual herb growing 1-3 feet tall with a bushy, upright growth habit and distinctive aromatic foliage.
description.roots: Fibrous root system with a central taproot extending 12-18 inches deep; numerous lateral roots for nutrient absorption.
description.stem: Square, hollow stems characteristic of the mint family; green to purple-tinged; branching extensively when pinched or harvested.
description.leaves: Opposite, ovate to elliptical leaves 2-4 inches long; smooth, glossy surface with prominent veins; bright green color with smooth or slightly serrated margins.
description.flowers: Small white, pink, or purple flowers arranged in terminal spikes; tubular shape typical of mint family; appear in late summer if allowed to flower.
description.fruits/seeds: Small, dark brown to black nutlets containing four seeds; produced after flowering but harvesting leaves before flowering maintains plant vigor.

traditionalUses: Respiratory ailments including coughs and bronchitis
Topical treatment for insect bites and minor wounds
preparations: 1-2 teaspoons dried leaves or 2-3 teaspoons fresh leaves per cup boiling water; steep 5-10 minutes
Not typically used for basil leaves due to volatile oil content
1:5 ratio with 40% alcohol; macerate for 2-4 weeks
Fresh crushed leaves applied directly to insect bites or minor cuts
Fresh leaves in carrier oil for 2-4 weeks; strain for topical use

Caution with bleeding disorders due to eugenol content
Avoid before surgery due to potential blood-thinning effects
sideEffects: Large doses may cause digestive upset
Skin sensitivity possible with concentrated essential oil
drugInteractions: May enhance effects of blood-thinning medications
Caution with sedative medications due to calming effects

name: Birch
nativeRange: Northern Hemisphere temperate and subarctic regions
habitat: Moist soils; forest edges; stream banks; pioneer species in disturbed areas

description.overall appearance: Deciduous trees reaching 40-70 feet tall with distinctive white, papery bark and catkin flowers.
description.roots: Shallow, spreading root system; forms symbiotic relationships with mycorrhizal fungi.
description.stem: Distinctive bark varies by species: white and papery (paper birch), yellow-bronze (yellow birch), or dark (river birch).
description.leaves: Alternate, serrated leaves 2-4 inches long; triangular to oval shape; bright green turning yellow in fall.
description.flowers: Wind-pollinated catkins; male catkins drooping, female catkins upright; appear before leaves.
description.fruits/seeds: Small, winged nutlets in cone-like structures; wind-dispersed; important food for birds.

compounds: Betulin and betulinic acid (triterpenes; anti-inflammatory, antiviral)
traditionalUses: Skin conditions including eczema and psoriasis
Urinary tract infections and kidney stones
modernUses: Urinary tract support and diuretic effects
preparations: 2-3 teaspoons dried leaves per cup; steep 10-15 minutes
Bark simmered 20-30 minutes for stronger effects
1:5 ratio with 50% alcohol; macerate bark or leaves 4 weeks
Fresh crushed leaves for topical anti-inflammatory use
Bark or leaves in carrier oil for topical pain relief
contraindications: Children with viral infections (Reye's syndrome risk)
sideEffects: Possible digestive upset with large doses
Skin sensitivity with topical use in some individuals

name: Black Cohosh
nativeRange: Eastern North America from southern Ontario to Georgia
habitat: Rich, moist deciduous forests and woodland edges; prefers shaded areas with organic-rich soils at elevations up to 3,000 feet

description.overall appearance: Tall perennial herb growing 3-8 feet tall with large, compound leaves and distinctive tall spikes of white, feathery flowers.
description.roots: Thick, dark, knotted rhizomes with fibrous roots; rhizomes are blackish-brown externally, whitish internally, with a bitter taste and unpleasant odor.
description.stem: Smooth, tall stems that are green to purplish, rising directly from the rhizome; stems can reach 6-8 feet in height during flowering.
description.leaves: Large, alternate, twice or thrice pinnately compound leaves with serrated leaflets; leaflets are 1-3 inches long and dark green with prominent veining.
description.flowers: Small, white flowers lacking petals but with numerous conspicuous stamens, arranged in tall, dense, bottlebrush-like racemes that can be 1-3 feet long.
description.fruits/seeds: Small, dry, pod-like follicles containing several small, flattened seeds; seeds have a scale-like appendage and are dispersed in late summer.

partsUsed: Occasionally aerial parts in traditional preparations
modernUses: Menopausal symptom relief (hot flashes, mood swings)
preparations: 1-2 teaspoons dried rhizome per cup water, simmer 15-20 minutes
1:5 ratio in 60% alcohol, 1-2 ml twice daily
20-40mg daily of extract standardized to triterpenes
1/2 teaspoon powdered root per cup hot water, steep 15 minutes
40-80mg standardized extract daily
contraindications: Pregnancy and breastfeeding (may stimulate uterine contractions)
drugInteractions: Hormone replacement therapy (may have additive effects)

name: Black Pepper
nativeRange: Western Ghats of India, particularly Kerala
habitat: Tropical rainforest regions; climbing on trees; humid, shaded environments

description.overall appearance: Perennial climbing vine reaching 12-20 feet in length; woody stems with nodes that produce roots for climbing support structures.
description.roots: Adventitious roots at nodes allow climbing; extensive fibrous root system in soil; aerial roots help attach to support trees.
description.stem: Woody, jointed stems with swollen nodes; green when young, becoming brown and woody; requires support structure for climbing.
description.leaves: Alternate, simple leaves 3-6 inches long; ovate to heart-shaped with prominent parallel veins; thick, leathery texture; dark green color.
description.flowers: Small, inconspicuous flowers arranged in drooping spikes 3-6 inches long; flowers lack petals; wind-pollinated.
description.fruits/seeds: Small drupes arranged in dense spikes; green when immature, turning red when ripe; dried immature fruits become black peppercorns.

traditionalUses: Respiratory conditions including coughs and asthma
modernUses: Bioavailability enhancement of other nutrients and drugs
preparations: 1/2 teaspoon ground pepper per cup hot water; steep 10 minutes
Whole peppercorns simmered 15-20 minutes for stronger effects
1:5 ratio with 70% alcohol; macerate crushed peppercorns 4 weeks
Ground pepper mixed with oil for topical pain relief
Peppercorns in carrier oil; strain after 2-4 weeks
sideEffects: Heartburn and acid reflux in sensitive individuals
Respiratory irritation if inhaled in large amounts
drugInteractions: Enhances absorption of many medications (beneficial and potentially problematic)

name: Blessed Thistle
nativeRange: Mediterranean region and southwestern Asia
habitat: Disturbed soils, roadsides, fields, and waste places in temperate climates

description.overall appearance: Blessed thistle is an annual herb growing 1-2 feet tall with a branching, spreading growth habit and distinctive spiny appearance.
description.roots: Shallow taproot system with fibrous secondary roots. Annual root system dies with the plant each year.
description.stem: Erect, branching stems are hairy, grooved, and often reddish-tinged. Stems become woody toward the base as the plant matures.
description.leaves: Deeply lobed, lance-shaped leaves are covered with soft spines and fine hairs. Leaves are pale green with prominent white veining.
description.flowers: Yellow flower heads are surrounded by spiny bracts and appear at stem tips from late spring through summer. Each head contains numerous small tubular flowers.
description.fruits/seeds: Small, brown achenes (seeds) are topped with bristly pappus that aids in wind dispersal. Seeds are produced abundantly.

compounds: Sesquiterpene lactones (cnicin - the primary bitter compound)
partsUsed: Aerial parts (leaves, stems, flowers) collected during flowering
preparations: 1-2 tsp dried herb per cup water, steep 10-15 minutes
Simmer for stronger bitter preparation
1:5 ratio in 40% alcohol, 1-3 ml three times daily
Fresh crushed leaves for external application
For topical skin applications
sideEffects: Nausea and vomiting in sensitive individuals
Contact dermatitis from handling fresh plant
drugInteractions: May enhance effects of diabetes medications
May affect absorption of other medications due to bitter compounds

name: Bloodroot
nativeRange: Eastern North America from Canada to Florida
habitat: Rich, moist deciduous forests; shaded woodland floors; well-drained humus soils

description.overall appearance: Perennial herb growing 6-10 inches tall with single lobed leaf and solitary white flower; red-orange rhizome.
description.roots: Thick, horizontal rhizome containing bright red-orange latex; bitter taste; spreads to form colonies.
description.stem: Single flowering stem emerges wrapped in the emerging leaf; no true above-ground stem.
description.leaves: Single, deeply lobed leaf 4-8 inches across; kidney-shaped with 5-9 rounded lobes; blue-green color.
description.flowers: Solitary white flower with 8-12 petals; 2-3 inches across; appears in early spring before leaves fully expand.
description.fruits/seeds: Elongated capsule containing black seeds with white appendages; ants disperse seeds for food reward.

traditionalUses: Skin conditions including warts and growths
modernUses: Dental care and oral health (very dilute preparations)
preparations: All preparations require extreme care due to toxicity
1:10 ratio with 70% alcohol; professional preparation only
Extremely dilute preparations for skin conditions
Commercial formulations only (not home preparation)
Standardized alkaloid content for scientific study
sideEffects: Severe skin irritation and burns with direct application

name: Blue Cohosh
nativeRange: Eastern North America, from southeastern Canada to northern Georgia
habitat: Rich, moist deciduous woodlands, often on slopes and ravines, in partial shade

description.overall appearance: Blue cohosh is a perennial woodland herb growing 1-3 feet tall with distinctive blue-purple stems and compound leaves. The plant has an elegant, upright growth habit.
description.roots: The rhizome is thick, knotted, and woody with numerous fibrous roots. Fresh rhizomes are yellowish-brown externally and whitish internally, becoming darker with age.
description.stem: Stems are smooth, erect, and often purplish or blue-tinted, especially when young. The stem is typically unbranched and hollow.
description.leaves: Large, compound leaves are divided into three leaflets, each further divided into 2-3 lobes. Leaves are blue-green with a waxy appearance and prominent veining.
description.flowers: Small, star-shaped flowers appear in terminal clusters in early spring before leaves fully develop. Flowers are yellow-green to purple-brown with 6 petals.
description.fruits/seeds: Distinctive bright blue, berry-like seeds develop in late summer. The seeds are exposed when the thin pericarp ruptures, creating the plant's characteristic blue "berries."

partsUsed: Rhizome and roots (collected in autumn after seed set)
traditionalUses: Labor induction and facilitation of childbirth
Menstrual regulation and relief of dysmenorrhea
modernUses: Uterine tonic (though controversial due to safety concerns)
Research into alkaloid properties for various conditions
preparations: 1 tsp dried root per cup water, steep 10-15 minutes
Simmer 1 oz root in 1 pint water for 20 minutes
1:5 ratio in 50% alcohol, 10-30 drops 2-3 times daily
Powdered root mixed with water for external application
Combined with other herbs for respiratory conditions
contraindications: Pregnancy (except under professional supervision for labor)
drugInteractions: May potentiate effects of cardiac medications
Possible interactions with blood pressure medications

name: Borage
nativeRange: Mediterranean region
habitat: Well-drained soils; sunny locations; disturbed areas; cultivated gardens

description.overall appearance: Annual herb growing 2-3 feet tall with hairy stems and leaves; star-shaped blue flowers.
description.roots: Taproot system extending 12-18 inches deep; fibrous lateral roots.
description.stem: Thick, hollow, branching stems covered with bristly hairs; succulent texture.
description.leaves: Large, oval leaves 3-6 inches long; rough, hairy texture; alternate arrangement; gray-green color.
description.flowers: Star-shaped blue flowers with 5 petals; pink buds opening to bright blue; drooping clusters.
description.fruits/seeds: Four small, dark nutlets; rich in gamma-linolenic acid (GLA); important for oil production.

compounds: Gamma-linolenic acid (GLA; 20-24% of seed oil; highest natural source)
Pyrrolizidine alkaloids (hepatotoxic; limit internal use of leaves)
preparations: Cold-pressed for highest GLA content
Fresh flowers steeped in hot water; beautiful blue color
LIMITED USE - 1 teaspoon dried leaves per cup (pyrrolizidine concern)
Fresh crushed leaves for topical inflammation
Seeds or flowers in carrier oil for topical use
contraindications: Pregnancy and breastfeeding (alkaloid concerns with leaves)
sideEffects: Liver toxicity with excessive leaf consumption
Skin irritation possible with topical use
drugInteractions: Anticoagulant medications (seed oil may enhance effects)

name: Burdock
nativeRange: Europe and Asia; naturalized in North America
habitat: Disturbed soils; roadsides; waste areas; rich, moist soil; partial shade to full sun

description.overall appearance: Biennial herb growing 3-8 feet tall in second year with large basal leaves and distinctive burr-like flower heads.
description.roots: Large, deep taproot extending 2-3 feet deep; brown exterior with white interior; fleshy and carrot-like.
description.stem: Tall, robust, branching stem in second year; hollow and ribbed; can reach 8 feet in height.
description.leaves: Very large basal leaves up to 18 inches long; heart-shaped with woolly undersides; smaller leaves alternate up stem.
description.flowers: Purple to pink thistle-like flowers in globular heads; surrounded by hooked bracts that form burrs; bloom in second year.
description.fruits/seeds: Brown achenes with pappus; dispersed by animals via burr attachment; inspired invention of Velcro.

partsUsed: Roots (primary medicinal use - first year roots preferred)
Young leaf stalks (culinary and mild medicinal)
traditionalUses: Skin conditions including eczema and psoriasis

1 tablespoon chopped fresh root per cup; simmer 20-30 minutes
1:5 ratio with 50% alcohol; macerate chopped fresh root 4-6 weeks
Fresh crushed leaves for topical skin applications
Fresh or dried root in carrier oil for topical skin care
contraindications: Known allergies to Asteraceae family plants
sideEffects: Possible allergic reactions in sensitive individuals
Contact dermatitis from fresh plant in sensitive people
drugInteractions: Diabetes medications (may enhance blood sugar lowering)

name: Calendula
nativeRange: Mediterranean region, likely southern Europe
habitat: Gardens, fields, waste places; adaptable to various soil conditions in temperate climates

description.overall appearance: Annual herb growing 12-24 inches tall with branching stems, aromatic leaves, and bright orange to yellow daisy-like flowers.
description.roots: Shallow taproot with fibrous secondary roots spreading near the soil surface.
description.stem: Erect, branching stems that are angular, slightly hairy, and pale green, reaching 12-24 inches in height.
description.leaves: Alternate, oblong to spatulate leaves 2-6 inches long with entire or slightly toothed margins, pale green and slightly sticky to touch.
description.flowers: Large composite flower heads 1.5-3 inches across with bright orange to yellow ray petals surrounding a darker central disc; blooms continuously from spring to frost.
description.fruits/seeds: Curved, boat-shaped achenes with a rough, bumpy surface; seeds are brown and distinctive in shape.

traditionalUses: European folk medicine for wound healing, skin conditions
Treatment of cuts, burns, bruises, and inflammation
preparations: 1-2 teaspoons dried petals per cup boiling water, steep 10-15 minutes
Fresh petals in olive oil for 2-4 weeks, strain for topical use
1:5 ratio in 40% alcohol, 2-4 ml three times daily
Oil infusion combined with beeswax for topical applications
Strong tea applied to affected areas with clean cloth
contraindications: Known allergy to plants in Asteraceae family
sideEffects: Rare allergic reactions in sensitive individuals
Contact dermatitis with topical use in allergic persons
Mild sedative effects with large internal doses

name: Cardamom
nativeRange: Western Ghats of Southern India and Sri Lanka
habitat: Tropical mountain forests; shaded understory; rich, humus soils

description.overall appearance: Perennial herbaceous plant growing 6-10 feet tall with leafy shoots arising from underground rhizomes; prefers forest understory.
description.roots: Thick, aromatic rhizome system with fibrous roots; rhizomes branch extensively and can be divided for propagation.
description.stem: Erect, reed-like pseudostems formed by overlapping leaf sheaths; true stems are underground rhizomes; shoots die back after fruiting.
description.leaves: Large, lance-shaped leaves 1-2 feet long; parallel venation typical of monocots; smooth, bright green with prominent midrib.
description.flowers: Small, white flowers with purple-striped lip on separate flowering shoots; arise directly from rhizome near ground level; orchid-like appearance.
description.fruits/seeds: Three-chambered pods containing 15-20 aromatic black seeds; pods green when harvested, turning brown when dried; seeds are the primary spice.

Urinary tract infections and kidney stones
preparations: 6-8 crushed pods or 1 teaspoon ground seeds per cup; steep 10-15 minutes
Whole pods simmered 15-20 minutes for stronger extraction

Ground seeds mixed with water for topical antimicrobial use
Seeds in carrier oil; avoid direct application due to potency

name: Cat's Claw
nativeRange: Amazon rainforest of Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, and Brazil
habitat: Primary and secondary rainforest, climbing high into the canopy on large trees

description.overall appearance: Cat's claw is a woody vine that can climb 100 feet or more into rainforest canopy using its distinctive hook-like thorns.
description.roots: The root system includes both fibrous roots and the thick, woody main root. The root bark is the primary source of medicinal compounds.
description.stem: Woody, climbing stems can reach several inches in diameter. The vine is characterized by curved, claw-like thorns that help it climb.
description.leaves: Opposite, oval leaves are 3-5 inches long with prominent veins. Leaves are dark green and glossy with smooth margins.
description.flowers: Small, white to yellowish flowers appear in spherical clusters at branch tips. Flowers are tubular and fragrant.
description.fruits/seeds: Small capsules split to release numerous tiny seeds with feathery appendages for wind dispersal.

Simmer 1 tbsp bark in 3 cups water for 15 minutes
1:5 ratio in 50% alcohol, 1-3 ml three times daily
Standardized extract preparations
Ground bark mixed with water for topical use

May enhance effects of blood-thinning medications

name: Catnip
nativeRange: Europe, Asia, and parts of Africa, naturalized throughout North America
habitat: Waste places, roadsides, disturbed soils, and areas with good drainage and full sun

description.overall appearance: Catnip is an aromatic perennial herb growing 2-4 feet tall with a bushy, branching growth habit and distinctive gray-green foliage.
description.roots: Fibrous root system with short rhizomes that spread slowly to form clumps rather than aggressive colonies.
description.stem: Square stems characteristic of mint family, erect and branching, covered with fine white hairs giving a grayish appearance.
description.leaves: Opposite, heart-shaped leaves are 1-3 inches long with serrated margins. Leaves are gray-green and covered with fine hairs.
description.flowers: Small, white to pale pink tubular flowers are arranged in dense terminal spikes. Flowers have purple spots and are very attractive to bees.
description.fruits/seeds: Small, brown nutlets (typically 4 per flower) are smooth and oval-shaped.

1:5 ratio in 40% alcohol, 2-4 ml three times daily

Possible interactions with medications for liver conditions
Generally considered very safe with minimal interactions

name: Chamomile
nativeRange: Europe, western Asia, northern Africa
habitat: Fields, roadsides, waste places; prefers sandy, well-drained soils in temperate climates

description.overall appearance: Annual herb growing 8-24 inches tall with feathery, finely divided leaves and distinctive small daisy-like flowers with sweet apple-like fragrance.
description.roots: Shallow fibrous root system, small taproot with numerous fine rootlets spreading near soil surface.
description.stem: Erect, branching stems that are smooth, hollow, and light green, growing 8-24 inches tall with multiple flower heads.
description.leaves: Alternate, bipinnately divided into linear segments, giving a feathery appearance; leaves are 1-2 inches long, bright green, and aromatic when crushed.
description.flowers: Small composite flower heads 0.5-1 inch across with 10-20 white ray petals surrounding a prominent yellow disc; petals bend downward as flowers mature; blooms May through September.
description.fruits/seeds: Small, light brown achenes without pappus, about 1mm long; seeds are tiny and numerous.

traditionalUses: European folk medicine for digestive disorders, insomnia, anxiety
Children's remedy for colic, teething pain, restlessness
Wound healing and skin inflammation treatment

Rarely used for chamomile
1:5 ratio in 40% alcohol, 2-4 ml three times daily
Fresh or reconstituted dried flowers for skin applications

name: Chaste Tree
nativeRange: Mediterranean region and western Asia
habitat: Riverbanks, coastal areas, and rocky hillsides; thrives in well-drained soils and Mediterranean climates at elevations up to 3,000 feet

description.overall appearance: Large deciduous shrub or small tree growing 8-20 feet tall with aromatic, palmate leaves and long spikes of purple, blue, or white flowers.
description.roots: Deep taproot system with spreading lateral roots; roots are woody and help anchor the plant in sandy or rocky soils.
description.stem: Multiple woody stems with gray-brown bark; young branches are square in cross-section (characteristic of mint family) and covered with fine hairs.
description.leaves: Opposite, palmately compound leaves with 5-7 lance-shaped leaflets; leaflets are 2-4 inches long, gray-green above, whitish beneath with aromatic scent.
description.flowers: Small, tubular, fragrant flowers arranged in dense, upright spikes 4-12 inches long; flowers are typically purple-blue but can be pink or white.
description.fruits/seeds: Small, round, dark purple berries about 1/4 inch diameter; berries have a peppery taste and contain 4 small seeds surrounded by aromatic flesh.

traditionalUses: Male sexual health and prostate support
modernUses: Irregular menstrual cycles and luteal phase defects
preparations: 1-2 teaspoons dried berries per cup water, simmer 10-15 minutes
1:5 ratio in 60% alcohol, 2-5 ml daily in morning
